current_fact.fact_text: March 22, 1955, saw the world’s first official "World Meteorological Day" celebrated (later fixed to March 23), recognizing how understanding Earth’s atmosphere can save lives. Long before climate change became a household term, scientists were already quietly building the networks that watch our skies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

current_fact.match_reason: An observance about reading weather and atmosphere pairs with an episode that feels like drifting with changing skies—balearic warmth and subtle shifts, like a musical forecast in motion.

queue.fact_text: On March 22, 1950, the first World Health Organization malaria eradication pilot projects were proposed, targeting mosquitoes across entire regions rather than just treating patients. It was one of the earliest attempts to tackle a disease by reshaping an ecosystem at scale.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: A global health campaign that blurred lines between medicine, ecology, and engineering mirrors a genre that blurs musical boundaries, layering influences into a new, atmospheric whole.

queue.fact_text: On March 22, 1965, NASA’s Ranger 9 spacecraft deliberately crashed into the Moon after sending back thousands of detailed television images of its surface. It turned a final fall into a last burst of data, helping scientists scout possible landing sites for the Apollo missions.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A probe’s terminal dive into the Moon to scout future journeys resonates with an episode about 'Destination' and exploration—both are voyages that prepare the way for others to follow.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 1982, Space Shuttle Columbia lifted off for mission STS-3, the first shuttle flight to test extended time in orbit and thermal extremes. The crew even performed experiments with a test robot arm, quietly paving the way for the routine space operations we now take for granted.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal voyage into space pairs with an episode themed around reimagining 'space' as creative possibility and forward momentum.

published.fact_text: When cosmonaut Valeri Polyakov landed on March 22, 1995, he had spent an astonishing 438 continuous days aboard the Mir space station. He stayed aloft so long partly to prove that humans could physically and mentally endure the duration of a mission to Mars.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: Polyakov’s ultra-long stay in orbit evokes the idea of a 'slow planet' and extended time, mirrored by the episode’s unhurried, drifting soundscapes.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 1960, Arthur Schawlow and Charles Townes received the first patent for a laser, at a time when many saw it as a 'solution looking for a problem.' Their abstract physics experiment eventually reshaped medicine, communications, and even the way your music is read on a CD.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

published.match_reason: Both the laser and Auto-Tune are precision technologies that began as controversial tools and ended up quietly redefining how we see and hear the world.

published.fact_text: Space Shuttle Atlantis launched on March 22, 1996, for mission STS-76, ferrying astronauts to the Russian Mir space station. Tucked into the mission was a spacewalk that helped prepare for a future all-new outpost: the International Space Station.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A shuttle docking with an orbital outpost echoes the episode’s slow 'launch' from deep, atmospheric beginnings into a free, roaming dance across distant sonic landscapes.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 1963, The Beatles released their debut album "Please Please Me," recorded astonishingly in a single marathon day of studio work. What started as a modest attempt to capture their live act became a seismic moment that helped ignite Beatlemania.

episode.title: Morning Drops #30 - Star Treatment
episode.description: A celebration of change and transformation. Gaia traces the evolution of Arctic Monkeys - from raw energy to cinematic sound - reflecting on how artists, and all of us, grow and change through time. A journey through courage, curiosity, and the beauty of slowing down.
songs: Arctic Monkeys - There’d Better Be a Mirrorball

published.match_reason: The Beatles’ explosive first album mirrors another band’s long arc of evolution; both stories chart how a guitar group grows into a cultural force and experiments with sound over time.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 1957, a U.S. Air Force Boeing C-97 Stratofreighter vanished over the Pacific Ocean with 67 people on board, leaving no confirmed wreckage behind. The disappearance still lingers as a little-known Cold War mystery over one of Earth’s vastest stretches of water.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: An aircraft lost over the ocean connects to an episode built around water, depth, and disappearing below the surface, translating tragedy into a gentler meditation on immersion and mystery.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 1508, Ferdinand II of Aragon appointed explorer Amerigo Vespucci as chief navigator of the Spanish Empire. The man whose name would label two entire continents was officially tasked with charting routes through oceans he had only just begun to understand.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: A royal commission to map the world resonates with an episode about travel, landscapes, and seafaring songs—modern sonic explorers echoing Vespucci’s navigational legacy.

published.fact_text: Comet Hale–Bopp made its closest pass to Earth on March 22, 1997, yet still remained over 1.3 astronomical units away—farther than the distance from Earth to the Sun. It was so bright that more than half the planet’s population could see it with the naked eye, turning the night sky into a shared global spectacle.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

published.match_reason: A brilliant comet lighting up Earth’s sky fits naturally with a 'Cosmic' themed mix, where spacey disco and electronics echo the sense of celestial brightness and shared wonder.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 1895, Auguste and Louis Lumière gave one of the first public demonstrations of their motion picture system in Paris. Their short, flickering scenes—workers leaving a factory, a train arriving—were so vivid that some early viewers reportedly flinched as if the train might burst out of the screen.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: The birth of cinema as a new storytelling medium aligns with an episode tracing the evolution of flamenco—another deeply visual, performative art that moved from small rooms to global stages and film screens.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 1621, the Pilgrims of Plymouth Colony signed a peace treaty with the Wampanoag leader Massasoit, with Squanto interpreting between worlds. For several decades the agreement held, a fragile moment of cooperation before later conflicts reshaped the continent.

episode.title: Morning Drops #06 - Friend's Home
episode.description: Yasmin and Gaia are reunited under the same roof to spend Easter Monday morning together, exploring that heartwarming 'just like home' feeling that comes from being surrounded by beloved friends.

published.match_reason: A rare period of peace and shared survival between communities mirrors an episode built around warmth, home, and the comfort of being together under one roof.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 2019, two buses collided near Kitampo in Ghana, killing at least 50 people along a major highway. It was one of the country’s deadliest road accidents, sparking renewed calls for safer transport on routes that knit together everyday life in West Africa.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: A tragedy on Ghanaian roads connects poignantly with an episode celebrating West African sounds, including Ghanaian highlife, honoring the region’s resilience and culture.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 2004, Hamas co-founder Ahmed Yassin was killed by an Israeli helicopter strike as he left a mosque in Gaza, along with his bodyguards and bystanders. The assassination sent shockwaves through the region, underlining how a single figure can become a symbol in a long, painful conflict.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: The killing of a polarizing political and religious figure connects with an episode about a fiercely political artist; both explore how individuals become symbols in conflicts much larger than themselves.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 1975, a fire broke out at the Browns Ferry Nuclear Power Plant in Alabama when a worker’s candle used for checking air leaks ignited foam in a cable tray. The accident, which nearly compromised the plant’s cooling systems, led to sweeping changes in nuclear safety rules worldwide.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A near-disaster at a nuclear plant—modern fire meeting controlled atoms—pairs with an 'Electric Shock' episode full of high-voltage guitars, echoing both danger and raw energy.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 1942, British and Italian warships clashed in the Second Battle of Sirte in the Mediterranean Sea, fighting over vital supply routes to North Africa. Despite heavy shelling, the British convoy squeezed through, showing how control of a single stretch of water can alter the course of a wider war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A naval battle in the Mediterranean over critical sea lanes ties thematically to an episode sailing peacefully across the same sea, replacing wartime convoys with musical voyages.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 1668, the privateer Henry Morgan landed in Cuba to raid the inland town of Puerto del Príncipe, marching his men through dense terrain to surprise the locals. Morgan operated with a government commission, walking the blurred line between pirate and official warfighter in the Caribbean.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A notorious privateer sailing Caribbean waters connects neatly with an episode literally made from the deck of a boat, trading plunder and raids for friendship and shared songs at sea.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 1622, Powhatan warriors launched a coordinated attack on English settlements around Jamestown, killing about a third of the colonists in a single day. To the English it was a massacre; to the Powhatan, it was a desperate attempt to push back an expansion that was reshaping their homelands.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: An explosive act of resistance against colonial expansion matches the raw, confrontational energy of Rage Against the Machine, a band built around protest and asymmetrical power.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 1993, Intel’s first Pentium processors began shipping, boasting around 100 million instructions per second and a 64‑bit data path—numbers that seemed almost futuristic at the time. The Pentium became the brain of a generation of home computers, quietly ushering in the modern internet age.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: The debut of a landmark computer chip that powered our digital lives ties directly to an episode about our dependence on modern devices and the psychology of being always connected.

published.fact_text: In the year 106, March 22 marked the start of the Bostran era, a dating system created for the new Roman province of Arabia Petraea. This regional calendar quietly ticked alongside the better-known Roman dates, a reminder that even timekeeping can be tailored to local identity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

published.match_reason: A lesser-known calendar era reflects how different cultures shape their own sense of time—echoing an episode that celebrates alternative rhythms, imperfections, and personal ways of marking moments.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 2017, U.S. helicopters airlifted about 500 members of the Syrian Democratic Forces across the Euphrates River, launching the Battle of Tabqa. It was a bold move in a complex civil war, showing how rivers that once nurtured ancient civilizations have become front lines in modern conflicts.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: An airlifted offensive and urban battlefields echo the themes of combat, discipline, and physical intensity in an episode rooted in boxing and martial arts training.

published.fact_text: On March 22, 2024, attackers carried out a horrific assault on Crocus City Hall near Moscow, combining gunfire and explosions during a concert and killing at least 145 people. A place built for music and gathering became, for a night, a scene of chaos that shocked audiences far beyond Russia.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A devastating attack in a concert hall demands a gentle, reflective response; this episode’s theme of storms passing and hopeful resilience offers a soft counterpoint to violence in musical spaces.

published.fact_text: Although not tied to a single year in your list, March 22 is also observed globally as World Water Day, highlighting that nearly one in four people still lack safely managed drinking water. It’s a reminder that something as elemental as clean water remains one of our era’s great unfinished tasks.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A day dedicated to water and its scarcity pairs naturally with an episode immersed in Arctic seas and marine life, translating a global issue into intimate, oceanic storytelling.

published.fact_text: March 22, 1955, saw the world’s first official "World Meteorological Day" celebrated (later fixed to March 23), recognizing how understanding Earth’s atmosphere can save lives. Long before climate change became a household term, scientists were already quietly building the networks that watch our skies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: An observance about reading weather and atmosphere pairs with an episode that feels like drifting with changing skies—balearic warmth and subtle shifts, like a musical forecast in motion.
